turns.content: I'm trying to migrate some performance-critical game logic into our new Rust codebase, and I've hit a wall on the API surface. My current JSON wrapper uses serde_json::Value for dynamic data structures, but when I try to bump the minor version to add a new field, cargo-semver-checks flags it as a major breaking change even though it feels like an additive update. Can someone explain why this is happening?

The issue stems from how semver detects breaking changes in public APIs. When you add a field to a struct or modify a method signature, cargo-semver-checks interprets that as potentially breaking existing consumers who rely on the old structure. Think of it like adding a mandatory column to an Excel sheet; if your clients were writing formulas assuming certain columns existed and others didn't, suddenly changing what is required breaks their code even if you aren't removing anything. The tool checks for any change in public visibility or signature that could affect downstream users.

But wait, I only added a field and kept all existing fields optional, so how does that break things? My clients shouldn't care if I add more data. And also, why did my #[doc(hidden)] comment on an internal helper struct still trigger the failure?

You're right that adding fields alone isn't always breaking for well-designed APIs, but cargo-semver-checks runs strict checks because in Rust, even small signature changes can break serialization logic or deserialization patterns. As for the #[doc(hidden)] comment, while it hides items from generated docs, certain operations like macro expansion or trait implementations might still expose them to external inspection depending on visibility modifiers and where they are used. The tool scans all public APIs regardless of whether they have #[doc(hidden)], so if that struct is part of a larger public type's implementation details, it gets flagged.
I keep getting false positives from my semver check runs and I suspect #[doc(hidden)] is the culprit. Why does hiding a field in docs break compatibility even if it's not public?

That is a classic trap with cargo-semver-checks. When you mark something with #[doc(hidden)], the tool still counts it as part of your public API surface because semver considers any documented item, regardless of visibility attributes like #[cfg(test)] or #[doc(hidden)]. Think of it like a secret room in your house; even if no one walks through the door to enter it, the fact that you built the walls and floor means the room exists structurally. If someone adds documentation about that hidden field tomorrow, the tool sees a new public entry point and flags it as a breaking change. The solution is usually to exclude those items from the API report entirely by using #[doc(hidden)] combined with #[cfg(feature = "internal")] or simply avoid documenting them at all so they never appear in the generated report. This prevents the false positive while keeping your internal implementation details private.

I am following along with a blog post about detecting breaking changes in our library. I tried using the standard doc comments to mark items as hidden, but then ran into issues because some items were still showing up in the public API even though they should be invisible. Here is the code snippet from the post: pub mod internal { #[doc(hidden)] pub fn secret_calc() {} }

It sounds like you are hitting a common gotcha where #[doc(hidden)] does not automatically remove an item from the stable public API surface. Think of it like putting a sticky note on a door in your house; if someone is looking through the peephole, they can still see that there is something behind the door even if the note says 'do not disturb.' In Rust terms, #[doc(hidden)] only hides items from the documentation generated by `cargo doc` and IDEs, but it does not prevent them from being exported or included in binary size calculations. You need to actually remove the item from the module using `#[cfg(feature = "internal")]` or move the entire module into a private scope that is never re-exported through your crate's public API. The key concept here is distinguishing between visibility for humans (documentation) and visibility for machines (the compiled binary), because cargo-semver-checks cares about what actually reaches users, not just what appears in help text.

Ah, so the distinction is that documentation is for people reading manuals while the API surface is what gets shipped to customers? That makes sense. I'm confused though because my Cargo.toml has a feature flag set up, but cargo-semver-checks says there are breaking changes when I run it in CI even though I thought those items were already gated.

Exactly, and the feature gate alone isn't enough if that item is still reachable through re-exports. Imagine you have a warehouse where some boxes are locked behind a security door (the feature flag). If someone else can walk into your main office and grab those boxes without going through the security door, then from their perspective, they still have access to them. This happens when you do `pub use` on something inside a gated module; it acts like an open gate in your warehouse that bypasses the security check entirely. You need to ensure no public re-exports point to items that rely on internal feature flags unless you intend for those features to be part of the stable contract. This is why cargo-semver-checks runs in CI as a safety net: it compares the current API against previous versions to catch any item that slipped through the cracks and became visible unexpectedly.

That analogy helps visualize how public re-exports bypass feature gates. But I'm still stuck on running this check in our CI pipeline. How do we configure the build script so it compares against the previous version's API surface automatically? Can you show me a concrete example of how to set up cargo-semver-checks with proper flags?

To automate this detection, you need to configure your CI runner to execute `cargo semver-checks --package your-crate-name` against the previous release's data. Think of it like running a physical comparison between two sets of blueprints before signing off on construction; if any new walls or windows appear where there shouldn't be ones in the old design, you must stop immediately. In practice, you add a step to your CI workflow that fetches the last published crate version from crates.io, then runs semver-checks with flags like `--package` and optionally `--release-version` to compare against the specific tag. This ensures that any item you accidentally exposed through re-exports or removed without updating documentation will trigger a failure before your library ships.

Okay, so I need to fetch the previous version's data specifically? And if cargo-semver-checks fails because of those hidden items I couldn't catch earlier, what specific error messages should I look for in the output?

Yes, you must explicitly target the exact crate and version hash from your registry. When semver-checks detects breaking changes due to #[doc(hidden)] pitfalls or accidental re-exports, it will report specific categories like 'public items removed', 'items added without documentation', or 'traits changed'. The error output will list the exact path of the broken item, such as `internal::secret_calc`, showing precisely where your visibility logic failed. This granularity allows you to fix the code by removing the public re-export or applying proper conditional compilation flags before the next release.

Why does the API keep breaking silently until someone complains? I thought once you removed a public field from my struct, it was safe because Rust guarantees stability, but now my tests are failing and I can't figure out why the old code just stopped working without any explicit error message.

It feels like Rust should guarantee that removing a public field is harmless since the type system enforces invariants, but in reality removing an exported field breaks compatibility because it changes the shape of the data consumers expect. The real issue here is that you likely relied on #[doc(hidden)] to protect something internal, thinking those fields are truly private and won't affect downstream users, but if those hidden items were actually being used by your crate's public API or exposed through re-exports, they still count as part of the stable interface. You probably assumed #[doc(hidden)] makes a field invisible to semver checks, but cargo-semver-checks will flag it anyway because the documentation structure itself is part of what gets checked for stability.

That sounds scary, I thought doc(hidden) was like a magic shield that ignores all breaking change detectors. So if I use serde_json::Value or any other crate to parse my output, they'll still see the hidden fields?

No, #[doc(hidden)] is not a magic shield because it doesn't prevent tools from inspecting your public API surface; it only suppresses those items from appearing in generated documentation but semver-checks will still detect them as breaking changes if they are part of the crate's stable interface. If you use serde_json::Value or any other crate to parse your output, they won't automatically see hidden fields unless you explicitly re-exported them or made them public again; however, removing a field that was previously public and then trying to hide it with #[doc(hidden)] doesn't stop existing clients from breaking their code because they are already compiled against the old signature. The fix is to ensure no public items are relied upon by external crates before you run cargo-semver-checks in your CI pipeline.
It feels like semver checking is just a fancy way of running cargo check, right? I thought the compiler would catch everything that breaks compatibility automatically.

That's a common assumption, but think of it like this: your compiler checks if you're using valid Rust syntax and rules, whereas cargo-semver-checks acts more like an auditor reviewing the documentation and API surface to ensure nothing was accidentally exposed or renamed in a way that breaks downstream users. The compiler cares about type safety; semver checking cares about contract stability.
Ah okay, so it's not just about code but also docs? I guess I thought only removing a public function would break things, like deleting an export from a module.

Exactly, and that's where the #[doc(hidden)] attribute becomes tricky. If you remove a public item or change its signature without marking it properly, cargo-semver-checks will flag it as a breaking change before your crate gets published to crates.io. It helps catch those subtle API drifts.
Got it, so I should run this in my CI pipeline like how we do cargo test? Just to make sure nothing slips through?

That's the right approach for most projects now. You can integrate semver checking into your GitHub Actions or GitLab CI workflows alongside normal compilation steps. It runs fast and gives you a detailed report of what changed in each version, including minor and major breaking changes.
One thing I noticed is that sometimes it complains about things like `#[doc(hidden)]` being missing when they should be there? Is that a false positive or real issue?

It's a real signal. If you accidentally expose an item without the #[doc(hidden)] tag, downstream users might rely on its documentation, and changing it breaks them. The tool flags this because it wants to ensure your public API remains stable even if internal details shift.

I've read about how to use the semver checking tool in my CI pipeline but I still don't get why the #[doc(hidden)] attribute is causing such frequent false positives. I thought hiding documentation would just mean those items wouldn't show up in the help output, so I assumed they were safe from breaking change detection.

The issue arises because cargo-semver-checks scans all public API surfaces including hidden documentation to verify consistency across versions. When you mark an item with #[doc(hidden)], it removes visibility from the normal docs but not necessarily from the internal API structure that the tool checks. If you remove or change such a hidden item in a minor version, the tool will flag this as a breaking change because the public contract technically changed even though it wasn't visible to users. This is why you need to be careful with #[doc(hidden)] items when planning your release strategy.

Ah okay so I can just ignore those hidden warnings? Or do I actually have to keep them consistent? I was thinking that since nobody sees the docs, nobody cares about their API stability so maybe I can refactor them freely without triggering the semver checks in my CI.

You cannot simply ignore those warnings because cargo-semver-checks is designed specifically to catch exactly this scenario where hidden items cause breaking changes. If you change or remove a #[doc(hidden)] item between major versions, that counts as a breaking change and will fail your check unless you explicitly mark it as intentional with the appropriate annotations. The tool treats hidden items as part of the public API surface for the purposes of semver checking to prevent accidental exposure of unstable features in future releases.

That makes sense but I'm confused about the exact configuration needed. Should I just run cargo-semver-checks --check-versions with some flags? And what happens if my CI pipeline fails because of these hidden item warnings?

You should run cargo-semver-checks --check-versions without any extra flags to get the default behavior which checks for breaking changes. If your CI fails due to #[doc(hidden)] items, you have two main options: either keep those items stable across major versions by documenting their intended lifetime, or explicitly mark them as intentional removals using the appropriate comment annotations in the code. This way the tool understands that these are deliberate design decisions rather than accidental regressions.

I think I understand now but can you clarify something about the output format? When the check fails due to hidden items, does it tell me exactly which item is problematic and what version introduced the change? And how do I differentiate between a real breaking change versus an intentional one in the reports?

Yes, cargo-semver-checks provides detailed output showing exactly which item caused the failure along with the specific versions where the change occurred. For hidden items specifically, it will highlight whether they were removed or modified and indicate if this violates semver rules for your crate's versioning strategy. To differentiate between real breaking changes and intentional ones, you need to carefully review the tool's output messages which categorize each failure type. Intentional removals of #[doc(hidden)] items should be documented with clear comments explaining why they were removed or changed in that particular release.

Thanks for clarifying but I'm still confused about how this integrates into my actual workflow. Should I just run these checks manually before every commit, or is there a better way to handle this? And what if I want to allow certain types of breaking changes like removing #[doc(hidden)] items in minor releases?

For your workflow, you should configure your CI pipeline to automatically run cargo-semver-checks as part of the build process rather than doing it manually. You can set up a GitHub Actions or GitLab CI job that triggers on every push to main branch and fails if semver checks don't pass. If you want to allow specific breaking changes like removing #[doc(hidden)] items in minor releases, you need to configure your versioning policy accordingly using the appropriate annotations or flags in cargo-semver-checks configuration files. This way you maintain strict control over which types of API changes are permitted at each release tier.
